source_paper_title: Popular Maximum-Utility Matchings with Matroid Constraints
context:
Page 6, Section 1.2 (Hardness results paragraph immediately before Theorem 4)

For the one-sided preferences model, we demonstrate that this problem is as hard as the exact matching problem [46] (see Section 5.1 for the definition), for which the existence of a deterministic efficient algorithm remains a longstanding open question.
title: Deterministic polynomial-time solvability of the Exact Matching problem
background_latex:
- Let \(G=(A,B;E)\) be a bipartite graph with \(|A|=|B|\).
- Each edge \(e\in E\) is colored either red or blue.
- For an edge set \(M\subseteq E\), let \(|M\cap E_{\mathrm{red}}|\) denote the number of red edges in \(M\), where \(E_{\mathrm{red}}\subseteq E\) is the set of red edges.
- A perfect matching is a matching \(M\subseteq E\) that covers every vertex in \(A\cup B\).
problem_definition_latex: Design a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm that, given \((G,k)\) as above and an integer \(k\), decides whether \(G\) admits a perfect matching \(M\) with exactly \(|M\cap E_{\mathrm{red}}|=k\) (equivalently, exactly \(k\) red edges), and outputs such an \(M\) if it exists.
latex:
- Let \(G=(A,B;E)\) be a bipartite graph with \(|A|=|B|\).
- Each edge \(e\in E\) is colored either red or blue.
- For an edge set \(M\subseteq E\), let \(|M\cap E_{\mathrm{red}}|\) denote the number of red edges in \(M\), where \(E_{\mathrm{red}}\subseteq E\) is the set of red edges.
- A perfect matching is a matching \(M\subseteq E\) that covers every vertex in \(A\cup B\).

Design a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm that, given \((G,k)\) as above and an integer \(k\), decides whether \(G\) admits a perfect matching \(M\) with exactly \(|M\cap E_{\mathrm{red}}|=k\) (equivalently, exactly \(k\) red edges), and outputs such an \(M\) if it exists.
location_in_paper: Page 6, Section 1.2 (Hardness results paragraph immediately before Theorem 4)
open_problem_quote: For the one-sided preferences model, we demonstrate that this problem is as hard as the exact matching problem [46] (see Section 5.1 for the definition), for which the existence of a deterministic efficient algorithm remains a longstanding open question.

verification.notice: This entry was generated from the paper text only. It has not been independently verified as still open. If you believe the question is solved or incorrectly stated, please contact the maintainers.
